text: In the case of left lower lobectomy, as in the right side, the inferior ligament is divided and the inferior ligament nodes are removed.
The pleura is divided anteriorly and posteriorly.
It is important to remove the hilar lymph nodes in the anterior and posterior aspect, again, to facilitate dissection and isolation of the hilar structures.
Once again, this could be done using a combination of blunt and cautery dissection.
Once the lymph nodes are removed, attention can then be paid in the anterior fissure.
You can see a prior wedge resection of the lower lobe lesion has been done.
In this instance, the fissure is not complete and
The fissure is developed using electrocautery.
Retraction can be provided by the natural lie of the lung as well as by the operating surgeon with the left hand cardiac forceps.
3D vision facilitates dissection of the proper plane to avoid parenchymal lung injury.
Dissection of the basal pulmonary artery and interlobar lymph nodes will expose the
space between the inferior hilum and the basilar artery allowing for completion of the anterior portion of the fissure.
In this case a portion is divided with the endovascular stapler and a portion is clipped and divided using an electrocautery.
The clip is placed on the proximal portion or the portion that is going to be staying to facilitate prevention of air leak.
Once the fissure is divided, the inferior pulmonary vein can be isolated.
Once again, division of the pleural posterior and anteriorly facilitates this.
Division of the inferior vein can be performed by passage of the endovascular stapler through the utility incision.
In this instance, the superior segment vein was divided separately.
Dissection is continued by lifting the lung superiorly by the assistant.
Once again, the regional and mediastinal lymph nodes are removed.
The lower lobe bronchus is then isolated and divided with a single fire of an endovascular stapler through the utility incision.
Once the vein and bronchus have been divided, the only thing remaining is the left lower low basal pulmonary artery.
In this instance, the fissure posteriorly is completed with electrocautery.
Alternatively, the fissure can be stapled at the conclusion of the procedure following isolation of the artery.
The artery is then stapled using a single fire in the endovascular stapler through the utility incision, resulting in complete detachment of the lower lobe.
